artifact_text:
# inflection.py — Public Function Summaries

## camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string into CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g. `"deviceType"`). It works by finding each character that follows a word boundary or underscore and uppercasing it, making it roughly the inverse of `underscore`, though the two are not perfect inverses in all cases (e.g. `"IOError"` round-trips as `"IoError"`).

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen and returns the result. It is a thin wrapper around `str.replace` with no case conversion or other transformation, so `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`.

## humanize

Turns a machine-style identifier into a readable English phrase. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix if present, replaces remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases the entire string, and then capitalises the very first character. The result is suitable for display in UI labels or headings, e.g. `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"` and `"author_id"` → `"Author"`.

## ordinal

Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for a given integer, following standard English rules. It handles the special teen cases (11, 12, 13 always get `"th"`) and works correctly with negative numbers by operating on the absolute value. For example, `ordinal(1)` returns `"st"` and `ordinal(-11)` returns `"th"`.

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"1003rd"`, or `"-11th"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number with the result, preserving the sign for negative inputs.

## parameterize

Converts a string into a URL-safe "slug". It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to their ASCII approximations, then replaces any remaining non-alphanumeric characters (except hyphens and underscores) with the chosen separator (default `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and lowercases everything. For example, `"Donald E. Knuth"` becomes `"donald-e-knuth"`.

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word using a prioritised list of regex rules stored in `PLURALS`, plus a set of known uncountable words (`UNCOUNTABLES`) that are returned unchanged. Rules are tried in order and the first match wins, handling irregular forms (e.g. `"octopus"` → `"octopi"`), words already in plural form, and case-preserving transformations for CamelCase inputs.

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It checks the `UNCOUNTABLES` set first and returns the word unchanged if matched; otherwise it applies the first matching rule from the `SINGULARS` list. Handles irregular forms (e.g. `"octopi"` → `"octopus"`), already-singular words, and case-preserving transformations for CamelCase inputs.

## tableize

Converts a class or model name into the corresponding database table name, following Rails conventions. It first converts the word to underscore_case with `underscore` and then pluralises the result with `pluralize`. For example, `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

## titleize

Converts a string into title case suitable for display as a heading. It first applies `underscore` and `humanize` to normalise the input, then capitalises the first letter of every word. Handles CamelCase, underscore-separated, hyphen-separated, and plain space-separated inputs, e.g. `"TheManWithoutAPast"` → `"The Man Without A Past"` and `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` → `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`.

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII Unicode characters with their closest ASCII equivalents by applying Unicode NFKD normalisation (which decomposes characters into base letters and combining marks) and then dropping any bytes that cannot be encoded as ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently removed. For example, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"` and `"Ærøskøbing"` becomes `"rskbing"`.

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed string into lowercase underscore_case. It inserts underscores between runs of uppercase letters and at transitions from a lowercase letter or digit to an uppercase letter, converts hyphens to underscores, and lowercases the whole result. It is roughly the inverse of `camelize`, e.g. `"DeviceType"` → `"device_type"`.

artifact_text:
# camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string to CamelCase. With `uppercase_first_letter=True` (the default), the result is UpperCamelCase (e.g., `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`); with `False`, it produces lowerCamelCase (e.g., `"deviceType"`). It works by capitalizing the character following each underscore and removing the underscores, making it roughly the inverse of `underscore`, though round-tripping is not guaranteed for strings like `"IOError"`.

# dasherize

Replaces every underscore character in the input string with a hyphen and returns the result. It is a simple string substitution with no other transformation, so `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`.

# humanize

Converts an underscore-formatted identifier into a readable, sentence-style string. It strips a trailing `_id` suffix if present, replaces remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases all alphabetic content, and then capitalizes the very first character, producing output like `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"` or `"author_id"` → `"Author"`.

# ordinal

Returns the English ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for the given integer, following standard English rules — including the special case where numbers ending in 11, 12, or 13 always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit. The sign of the number is ignored (negative numbers are treated as their absolute value).

# ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, or `"1003rd"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with that suffix.

# parameterize

Transforms an arbitrary string into a URL-safe "slug". It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to ASCII approximations, then replaces any remaining non-alphanumeric characters (other than hyphens and underscores) with the given separator (default `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and finally lowercases the whole result.

# pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by testing it against a prioritized list of regex rules (`PLURALS`) and applying the first matching substitution. Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (such as `"sheep"` or `"information"`) are returned unchanged. The rules handle common irregular and regular patterns, including Latin/Greek-derived endings and words ending in sibilants.

# singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the inverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word matches any entry in `UNCOUNTABLES` and returns it unchanged if so. Otherwise it walks the `SINGULARS` rule list and applies the first matching regex substitution, handling irregular and regular patterns such as `"octopi"` → `"octopus"` and `"posts"` → `"post"`.

# tableize

Converts a class-style name (CamelCase or underscore) into a pluralized, underscored table name following Rails conventions. It does this by first calling `underscore` to produce a snake_case string and then calling `pluralize` on the result, so `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"`.

# titleize

Converts a string into a title-cased form suitable for display by capitalizing the first letter of every word. It normalizes the input by first running it through `underscore` and then `humanize` to expand camelCase and replace underscores with spaces, then capitalizes each word boundary, handling punctuation such as apostrophes correctly.

# transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII characters in a Unicode string with their closest ASCII equivalents using Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes characters into base letters plus combining diacritics) and then discards any bytes that still cannot be represented in ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently dropped, so `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"` and `"Ærøskøbing"` becomes `"rskbing"`.

# underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed-case identifier into a lowercase, underscore-separated string. It inserts an underscore between sequences of uppercase letters followed by a capitalized word, between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter, and also replaces hyphens with underscores, making it roughly the inverse of `camelize`.

artifact_text:
# inflection.py — Public Function Notes

## camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string to CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g. `"deviceType"`). It works by finding each character that either starts the string or follows an underscore and uppercasing it. It is the rough inverse of `underscore`, though the round-trip is not perfect for strings like `"IOError"`, which becomes `"IoError"` after the round-trip.

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen/dash. It is a simple one-liner wrapper around `str.replace` and is useful for converting underscore-style identifiers into dash-separated forms commonly found in URLs and CSS (e.g. `"puni_puni"` → `"puni-puni"`).

## humanize

Turns a database-style underscored identifier into a readable, human-friendly phrase. It strips a trailing `_id` suffix if present, replaces remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases the entire string, then capitalizes only the very first character. For example, `"employee_salary"` becomes `"Employee salary"` and `"author_id"` becomes `"Author"`.

## ordinal

Returns just the ordinal suffix string (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for a given integer, handling the special cases for 11, 12, and 13 (which always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit) and working correctly for negative numbers by taking the absolute value first.

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"1003rd"`, or `"-11th"`. It delegates the suffix logic to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with the result.

## parameterize

Converts a string into a URL-safe "slug" suitable for use in a pretty URL. It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to their ASCII approximations via `transliterate`, then replaces any remaining non-alphanumeric characters (other than hyphens and underscores) with the separator (defaulting to `"-"`), collapses runs of the separator into a single one, strips leading and trailing separators, and finally lowercases everything. For example, `"Donald E. Knuth"` becomes `"donald-e-knuth"`.

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by applying the first matching rule from the `PLURALS` rule list. Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (such as `"sheep"`, `"fish"`, or `"information"`) are returned unchanged. Rules are case-insensitive and also handle words already in their plural form, irregular plurals registered via `_irregular`, and common suffix patterns (e.g. `"octopus"` → `"octopi"`, `"CamelOctopus"` → `"CamelOctopi"`).

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word (or a portion of it) matches any entry in `UNCOUNTABLES` and returns it unchanged if so. Otherwise it walks the `SINGULARS` rule list and applies the first matching substitution (e.g. `"octopi"` → `"octopus"`, `"posts"` → `"post"`). Words with no matching rule are returned as-is.

## tableize

Generates a database table name from a class or model name using Rails conventions. It first converts the input to snake_case with `underscore`, then pluralizes the result with `pluralize`. For example, `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

## titleize

Converts a string into title case suitable for display as a heading. Internally it passes the string through `underscore` and then `humanize` to normalize it, then capitalizes every word. Dashes, underscores, and CamelCase word boundaries are all handled, so inputs like `"TheManWithoutAPast"`, `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"`, and `"x-men: the last stand"` all become properly capitalized title strings.

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII Unicode characters with their closest ASCII equivalents by applying Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes accented characters into base letter + combining mark) and then encoding to ASCII while dropping any characters that have no ASCII approximation. For example, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"`. Characters with no ASCII equivalent (such as the `Æ` ligature) are silently dropped.

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed string into a lowercased, underscore-separated form. It inserts underscores at boundaries between sequences of uppercase letters followed by a capitalized word, and between a lowercase letter or digit followed by an uppercase letter. It also converts hyphens to underscores. For example, `"DeviceType"` becomes `"device_type"`. It is the rough inverse of `camelize`, with the same caveat that the round-trip is not always perfect.

artifact_text:
# camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string to CamelCase. When `uppercase_first_letter` is `True` (the default), the result starts with a capital letter (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`); when `False`, the first letter is lowercased to produce lowerCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"deviceType"`). It works by replacing each underscore-preceded character with its uppercase equivalent and can be thought of as the inverse of `underscore`, though the round-trip is not always perfect (e.g. `"IOError"` becomes `"IoError"`).

# dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen-dash. It is a straightforward single-operation transform with no special-case logic — `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`.

# humanize

Transforms a database-style identifier into a readable English phrase. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix, converts remaining underscores to spaces, lowercases all alphabetic characters, then capitalizes the very first character. The result is suitable for display labels, e.g. `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"` and `"author_id"` → `"Author"`.

# ordinal

Returns the English ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for the given integer, correctly handling the special cases for 11, 12, and 13 which always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit. The sign of the number is ignored — `-1021` and `1021` both yield `"st"`.

# ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, or `"1003rd"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and simply prepends the original number (preserving its sign) to the returned suffix.

# parameterize

Converts a string into a URL-safe slug. It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to ASCII approximations via `transliterate`, then replaces any sequence of characters that are not alphanumeric, hyphens, or underscores with the separator (default `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and lowercases the result. For example, `"Donald E. Knuth"` becomes `"donald-e-knuth"`.

# pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by testing it against an ordered list of regex substitution rules (`PLURALS`). Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (such as `"sheep"` or `"information"`) are returned unchanged. The rules handle a wide range of English morphology including irregular endings like `-ix`/`-ices`, `-us`/`-i`, and consonant-`y` → `-ies`, and they are case-insensitive so CamelCased words like `"CamelOctopus"` are handled correctly.

# singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the inverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word is in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (matched as a whole word, case-insensitively) and returns it unchanged if so. Otherwise it walks the `SINGULARS` rule list in order, applying the first matching regex substitution and returning the result; if no rule matches the word is returned as-is.

# tableize

Produces a Rails-style database table name from a class or model name. It chains two operations: `underscore` to convert CamelCase to snake_case, then `pluralize` to put the last word in plural form. For example, `"RawScaledScorer"` → `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` → `"fancy_categories"`.

# titleize

Converts a string into a title-cased display string by first running it through `underscore` and `humanize` to normalize separators and casing, calling Python's `str.title()`, then applying a final regex pass to capitalize the first letter of every word (including those starting with an apostrophe). This correctly handles camelCase input, hyphenated strings, and underscore-separated identifiers, e.g. `"TheManWithoutAPast"` → `"The Man Without A Past"`.

# transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII Unicode characters with their closest ASCII equivalents by decomposing the string using Unicode NFKD normalization and then dropping any bytes that cannot be encoded as ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently removed. For example, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"` while characters like `"Æ"` that decompose to nothing ASCII-representable are dropped entirely.

# underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed-case string into a lowercase, underscore-separated (snake_case) string. It inserts underscores between sequences of uppercase letters followed by a capitalized word, between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter, and replaces hyphens with underscores before lowercasing the whole result. It is broadly the inverse of `camelize`, though the two are not perfect inverses for inputs like `"IOError"`.

artifact_text:
# inflection.py — Public Function Notes

## camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string into CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g. `"deviceType"`). It works by replacing each underscore followed by a character with the uppercased version of that character. It is roughly the inverse of `underscore`, though the round-trip is not perfect for strings like `"IOError"`, which would come back as `"IoError"`.

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore character in a string with a hyphen/dash. It is a thin wrapper around `str.replace` and performs no other transformation, so the casing and structure of the string are otherwise left intact (e.g. `"puni_puni"` → `"puni-puni"`).

## humanize

Turns a machine-style identifier into a readable English phrase. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix if present, replaces all remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases the entire string, and then capitalizes just the first character. The result is suitable for display in a UI where a raw database column name like `"employee_salary"` should read as `"Employee salary"` and `"author_id"` should read as `"Author"`.

## ordinal

Returns the correct English ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer without attaching it to the number. It handles the special teen cases 11–13 (which always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit) and works correctly for negative numbers by operating on the absolute value. The result is used internally by `ordinalize`.

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"1003rd"`, or `"-11th"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving sign) with the returned suffix.

## parameterize

Transforms an arbitrary string into a URL-safe "slug" suitable for use in a pretty URL. It first converts non-ASCII characters to their ASCII approximations via `transliterate`, then replaces any remaining characters that are not alphanumeric, hyphens, or underscores with the separator (a hyphen by default). Consecutive separators are collapsed into one, leading and trailing separators are stripped, and the whole result is lowercased (e.g. `"Donald E. Knuth"` → `"donald-e-knuth"`).

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word using a prioritized list of regex substitution rules (`PLURALS`) and a set of uncountable words (`UNCOUNTABLES`). If the word is empty or already in the uncountable set (e.g. `"sheep"`, `"information"`), it is returned unchanged. Otherwise the rules are tried in order and the first matching rule's substitution is applied, handling irregular forms such as `"octopus"` → `"octopi"` as well as standard patterns.

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word matches any entry in `UNCOUNTABLES` (case-insensitively) and returns it unchanged if so. Otherwise it works through the `SINGULARS` regex rule list in order, applying the first matching substitution to recover the singular form (e.g. `"octopi"` → `"octopus"`, `"posts"` → `"post"`).

## tableize

Converts a class or model name into the conventional plural snake_case table name used by Rails-style ORMs. It does this by first calling `underscore` to convert CamelCase to snake_case and then calling `pluralize` on the result (e.g. `"RawScaledScorer"` → `"raw_scaled_scorers"`, `"fancyCategory"` → `"fancy_categories"`).

## titleize

Formats a string as a title by capitalizing the first letter of every word. It normalizes the input first — running it through `underscore` to break apart CamelCase and then `humanize` to replace underscores with spaces — before capitalizing each word boundary. The result is intended for human-readable headings (e.g. `"TheManWithoutAPast"` → `"The Man Without A Past"`, `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` → `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`).

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII characters in a Unicode string with their closest ASCII equivalents, dropping any character for which no ASCII approximation exists. It achieves this via Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes characters into base letters plus combining marks) followed by ASCII encoding with `"ignore"` error handling, which discards the combining marks (e.g. `"älämölö"` → `"alamolo"`). It is used internally by `parameterize`.

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed-case string into a lowercase, underscore-separated (snake_case) string. It inserts underscores between sequences of uppercase letters followed by a lowercase letter, between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter, and converts any hyphens to underscores before lowercasing everything (e.g. `"DeviceType"` → `"device_type"`). It is roughly the inverse of `camelize`, though the round-trip is not lossless for all inputs.

artifact_text:
# camelize

Converts a snake_case string to CamelCase. When `uppercase_first_letter` is `True` (the default), the result is UpperCamelCase (e.g., `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`); when `False`, it produces lowerCamelCase (e.g., `"device_type"` → `"deviceType"`). It works by uppercasing the character after each underscore (and the first character if applicable) using a regex substitution.

# dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen/dash. This is a simple single-operation transform useful for converting snake_case identifiers into kebab-case, for example when generating CSS class names or URL path segments (e.g., `"puni_puni"` → `"puni-puni"`).

# humanize

Transforms a snake_case or database-style identifier into a human-readable string. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix if present, replaces underscores with spaces, lowercases the entire string, and then capitalizes the first letter, producing a sentence-style label suitable for display in UIs (e.g., `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"`, `"author_id"` → `"Author"`).

# ordinal

Returns the ordinal suffix string (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for a given integer, accounting for the special cases of 11th, 12th, and 13th which always use `"th"` regardless of their last digit. The input is converted to its absolute value before computing the suffix, so negative numbers are handled correctly (e.g., `-1021` yields `"st"`).

# ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string like `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"3rd"`, or `"1002nd"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal()` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with the result, making it convenient for rendering ranked or sequenced output.

# parameterize

Converts a string into a URL-safe "slug" by first transliterating non-ASCII characters to their ASCII approximations, then replacing any remaining non-alphanumeric characters (other than hyphens and underscores) with a configurable separator (default `"-"`), collapsing consecutive separators into one, trimming leading and trailing separators, and lowercasing the result (e.g., `"Donald E. Knuth"` → `"donald-e-knuth"`).

# pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by testing it against an ordered list of regex substitution rules (the `PLURALS` list) and applying the first matching rule. Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (such as `"sheep"` or `"fish"`) are returned unchanged. The rules handle a wide range of English morphological patterns including irregular forms registered via `_irregular`.

# singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word is uncountable (returning it unchanged if so), then applies the first matching rule from the `SINGULARS` list of regex substitutions. It handles irregular forms, Latin-derived plurals (e.g., `"octopi"` → `"octopus"`), and standard English plural endings.

# tableize

Generates a database table name from a class or model name using Rails conventions. It first converts the word to snake_case via `underscore` (handling CamelCase input), then pluralizes the result via `pluralize`, so that, for example, `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

# titleize

Converts a string into title case suitable for display, handling CamelCase, snake_case, kebab-case, and plain prose as input. It first underscores and humanizes the word to normalize it into a space-separated lowercase phrase, applies Python's `str.title()` for initial capitalization, then re-applies a regex to ensure possessives and edge cases are handled (e.g., `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` → `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`).

# transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII Unicode characters with their closest ASCII equivalents by first applying Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes characters into base letters and combining marks) and then encoding as ASCII while silently dropping any characters that have no ASCII representation. For example, accented Latin letters like `ä` become `a`, while characters with no ASCII approximation (like `Æ`) are dropped entirely.

# underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed-case string into a lowercased, underscore-separated (snake_case) string. It handles runs of uppercase letters followed by a capitalized word (e.g., `"XMLParser"` → `"xml_parser"`), transitions from lowercase to uppercase, and replaces hyphens with underscores, making it roughly the inverse of `camelize` (e.g., `"DeviceType"` → `"device_type"`).

artifact_text:
# inflection.py — Public Function Summaries

## camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string into CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g. `"deviceType"`). It works by finding each character that either starts the string or follows an underscore and uppercasing it, then stripping the underscores. It is roughly the inverse of `underscore`, though round-tripping through both functions is not always lossless (e.g. `"IOError"` becomes `"IoError"` after the round trip).

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore character in a string with a hyphen/dash. It is a thin wrapper around Python's `str.replace` and performs no other transformation, so case and all other characters are left untouched (e.g. `"puni_puni"` → `"puni-puni"`).

## humanize

Turns a machine-style identifier into a readable English phrase. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix if present, replaces remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases the entire string, and then capitalizes only the very first character. The result is suitable for display in user interfaces where a single leading capital and no underscores are desired (e.g. `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"`, `"author_id"` → `"Author"`).

## ordinal

Returns the ordinal suffix string (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for the given integer, following standard English rules. It handles the special cases for 11, 12, and 13 (which always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit) and works correctly for negative numbers by operating on the absolute value. The function returns only the suffix, not the number itself — use `ordinalize` to get the full string.

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a complete ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"1003rd"`, or `"-11th"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with the result, making it convenient for display whenever a position in a sequence needs to be expressed in natural English.

## parameterize

Transforms an arbitrary string into a URL-safe "slug". It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to their closest ASCII equivalents via `transliterate`, then replaces any remaining non-alphanumeric characters (other than hyphens and underscores) with the specified separator (default `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and finally lowercases the result (e.g. `"Donald E. Knuth"` → `"donald-e-knuth"`). A custom separator can be supplied, or an empty string to remove separators entirely.

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by working through a prioritized list of regular-expression substitution rules (`PLURALS`) and applying the first matching rule. Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (e.g. `"sheep"`, `"information"`) are returned unchanged, as is an empty string. Case in the original word is generally preserved through the regex back-references used in the replacement patterns.

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word matches any entry in `UNCOUNTABLES` (returning it unchanged if so), then applies the first matching rule from the `SINGULARS` list of regex substitutions. Words that are already singular and match no rule are returned as-is.

## tableize

Derives a database table name from a class or model name using Rails conventions: it first converts the word to `snake_case` via `underscore` and then pluralizes the result via `pluralize`. For example, `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

## titleize

Converts a string into title case suitable for display as a heading. It first normalizes the input by running it through `underscore` and then `humanize` to strip underscores, CamelCase boundaries, and `_id` suffixes, before capitalizing the first letter of every word. The result is human-readable with each word capitalized (e.g. `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` → `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`, `"TheManWithoutAPast"` → `"The Man Without A Past"`).

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII characters in a Unicode string with their closest ASCII approximations by applying Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes characters into base letters plus combining diacritics) and then discarding any byte that cannot be encoded as ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently dropped rather than replaced. For example, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"` and `"Ærøskøbing"` becomes `"rskbing"`.

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed string into a lowercase, underscore-separated form. It inserts an underscore between sequences of uppercase letters and a following capitalized word, between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter, and replaces any hyphens with underscores before lowercasing the whole string (e.g. `"DeviceType"` → `"device_type"`). It is approximately the inverse of `camelize`, though round-tripping through both is not always lossless.

# inflection.py — Public Function Summaries

## camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string into CamelCase. When `uppercase_first_letter` is `True` (the default), the result starts with a capital letter, producing UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`). When set to `False`, the first letter is lowercased to produce lowerCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"deviceType"`). It works by replacing each underscore-preceded character with its uppercase equivalent using a regex substitution.

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen/dash. It is a thin wrapper around `str.replace` with no special-case logic, so `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`.

## humanize

Transforms an underscore-separated identifier into a readable, sentence-style label. It strips a trailing `_id` suffix if present, converts remaining underscores to spaces, lowercases the whole string, then capitalizes only the first character — producing output like `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"` or `"author_id"` → `"Author"`.

## ordinal

Returns the ordinal suffix string (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for a given integer, following standard English rules including the special cases for 11th, 12th, and 13th. The sign of the number is ignored (absolute value is used), so `-1021` yields `"st"` just as `1021` does.

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, or `"1003rd"`. It delegates the suffix logic to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with the result.

## parameterize

Converts a string into a URL-friendly "slug" suitable for use in a pretty URL. It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to ASCII approximations, then replaces any remaining non-alphanumeric characters (other than hyphens and underscores) with the separator (defaulting to `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and lowercases the result — so `"Donald E. Knuth"` becomes `"donald-e-knuth"`.

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by testing it against the ordered list of regex rules in `PLURALS`, returning the word unchanged if it is in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set or is an empty string. The rules handle common irregular patterns (e.g. `"octopus"` → `"octopi"`) and preserve the original capitalization where the regex captures allow it.

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word matches any entry in `UNCOUNTABLES` (returning it unchanged if so), then walks the `SINGULARS` rule list in order, applying the first matching regex substitution — for example turning `"octopi"` back into `"octopus"` or `"posts"` into `"post"`.

## tableize

Produces a database table name from a class or model name in the style used by Ruby on Rails. It first converts the word to `snake_case` via `underscore`, then pluralizes the result with `pluralize`, so `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

## titleize

Converts a string into title case suitable for display as a heading. It passes the input through `underscore` and then `humanize` to normalize separators, then capitalizes the first letter of every word, handling inputs that are already CamelCase, hyphenated, or underscore-separated — for example `"TheManWithoutAPast"` → `"The Man Without A Past"`.

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII characters in a Unicode string with their closest ASCII equivalents by applying Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes characters into base letters plus combining marks) and then encoding to ASCII while dropping any characters that have no ASCII representation. For instance, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"` and characters with no ASCII approximation, like the `Æ` ligature, are silently dropped.

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed string into a lowercase, underscore-separated form. It inserts underscores between sequences of uppercase letters followed by a lowercase letter, between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter, and replaces hyphens with underscores, then lowercases everything — so `"DeviceType"` becomes `"device_type"`.

artifact_text:
# inflection.py — Public Function Notes

## camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string into CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g., `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g., `"DeviceType"`). It works by finding each character that follows a word boundary or an underscore and uppercasing it. It is the rough inverse of `underscore`, though the round-trip is not always lossless (e.g., `"IOError"` becomes `"IoError"` after a round-trip).

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen/dash. It is a simple one-for-one character substitution with no other transformation, so `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`. It does not alter casing or handle any other special characters.

## humanize

Turns a machine-style identifier into a readable, human-friendly label. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix if present, replaces remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases all alphabetic characters, and then capitalizes the very first character of the resulting string. For example, `"employee_salary"` becomes `"Employee salary"` and `"author_id"` becomes `"Author"`.

## ordinal

Returns just the ordinal suffix string (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for a given integer. It correctly handles the special cases for 11, 12, and 13 (which always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit), and it works on negative numbers by taking the absolute value first. The number itself is not included in the return value — only the suffix.

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"3rd"`, or `"1002nd"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and then concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with that suffix, so negative numbers like `-1021` correctly yield `"-1021st"`.

## parameterize

Transforms an arbitrary string into a URL-safe "slug" suitable for use in a pretty URL. It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to their ASCII approximations, then replaces any remaining non-alphanumeric characters (other than hyphens and underscores) with the separator (a hyphen by default). Consecutive separators are collapsed to one, leading and trailing separators are stripped, and the whole result is lowercased. For example, `"Donald E. Knuth"` becomes `"donald-e-knuth"`.

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word using a prioritized list of regex replacement rules stored in `PLURALS`, plus a set of known uncountable words (`UNCOUNTABLES`). If the word is empty or already in the uncountable set (e.g., `"sheep"`, `"information"`), it is returned unchanged. Otherwise the rules are tried in order and the first matching rule is applied, handling irregular forms (like `"octopus"` → `"octopi"`) and preserving the original casing of the word.

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word (or any suffix of it) matches an uncountable noun, returning it unchanged if so. Otherwise it walks the `SINGULARS` rule list in order and applies the first matching regex substitution, correctly handling irregular forms like `"octopi"` → `"octopus"` and `"CamelOctopi"` → `"CamelOctopus"`.

## tableize

Converts a class or model name into the conventional database table name by first underscoring it (via `underscore`) and then pluralizing the result (via `pluralize`). This mirrors Ruby on Rails' convention for deriving table names from model names, so `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

## titleize

Formats a string as a title by capitalizing the first letter of every word. It first converts the input through `underscore` and then `humanize` to normalize separators and casing, then applies Python's `str.title()` followed by a regex that capitalizes word-initial characters (including those preceded by an apostrophe). The result is a human-readable title such as `"man from the boondocks"` → `"Man From The Boondocks"` or `"TheManWithoutAPast"` → `"The Man Without A Past"`.

## transliterate

Converts a Unicode string to its closest ASCII representation by applying Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes characters into base letters and combining marks) and then discarding any byte that is not valid ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently dropped rather than replaced. For example, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"`, while characters like `"Æ"` that have no ASCII base are omitted entirely.

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed identifier string into a lowercase, underscore-separated form. It inserts underscores between sequences of uppercase letters and the following mixed-case word, between a lowercase letter (or digit) and an uppercase letter, and replaces any existing hyphens with underscores, before lowercasing the whole string. It is the rough inverse of `camelize`, so `"DeviceType"` becomes `"device_type"`, though the round-trip is not always lossless for strings like `"IOError"`.

# camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string to CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`); passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g. `"deviceType"`). It works by uppercasing the first character after every underscore (and the leading character when using the default), making it roughly the inverse of `underscore`, though round-tripping through both is not always lossless (e.g. `"IOError"` becomes `"IoError"`).

# dasherize

Replaces every underscore character in a string with a hyphen-dash, and returns the result. It is a simple one-to-one character substitution with no other transformations, so `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`.

# humanize

Converts a database-style identifier into a readable English phrase. It strips a trailing `_id` suffix if present, replaces remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases the entire string, then capitalizes the first letter—turning something like `"employee_salary"` into `"Employee salary"` or `"author_id"` into `"Author"`.

# ordinal

Returns the ordinal suffix string (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for a given integer, respecting the special-case teens (`11`, `12`, `13`) that always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit. The input is treated as an absolute value, so negative numbers work correctly (e.g. `-1021` → `"st"`).

# ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"1003rd"`, or `"-11th"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with the returned suffix.

# parameterize

Transforms an arbitrary string into a URL-safe "slug". It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to their ASCII approximations, then replaces any character that is not alphanumeric, a hyphen, or an underscore with the separator (default `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and lowercases the result—turning `"Donald E. Knuth"` into `"donald-e-knuth"`.

# pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word using a prioritized list of regex substitution rules, including special handling for irregular words (e.g. `"person"` → `"people"`) registered at module load time. Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (such as `"sheep"` or `"information"`) are returned unchanged, and case is preserved so that `"CamelOctopus"` becomes `"CamelOctopi"`.

# singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It checks the word against the `UNCOUNTABLES` set first and returns it unchanged if matched; otherwise it walks the `SINGULARS` regex rule list and applies the first matching substitution, correctly handling irregular and Latin-derived forms (e.g. `"octopi"` → `"octopus"`, `"CamelOctopi"` → `"CamelOctopus"`).

# tableize

Derives a database table name from a class or model name in the Rails convention: it first converts the word to underscore_case via `underscore`, then pluralizes the result via `pluralize`. For example, `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

# titleize

Converts a string into a title-cased human-readable heading by first underscoring and humanizing it, then capitalizing the first letter of every word. It handles camel-cased input (`"TheManWithoutAPast"` → `"The Man Without A Past"`), hyphenated input (`"x-men: the last stand"` → `"X Men: The Last Stand"`), and underscore-separated input (`"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` → `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`).

# transliterate

Converts a Unicode string to its closest ASCII representation by applying NFKD normalization (which decomposes characters into base letters plus combining marks) and then dropping all non-ASCII bytes. Characters that have no ASCII approximation are silently omitted, so `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"` while `"Ærøskøbing"` loses letters that can't be approximated and becomes `"rskbing"`.

# underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed string into a lowercase, underscore-separated form. It inserts underscores between sequences of uppercase letters followed by a capitalized word, between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter, and replaces hyphens with underscores before lowercasing everything—so `"DeviceType"` becomes `"device_type"`. It is roughly the inverse of `camelize`, with the same caveat that round-tripping is not always lossless.

artifact_text:
# inflection.py — Public Function Summaries

## camelize

Converts an underscored or dash-separated string into CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g. `"deviceType"`). It works by finding each character that immediately follows a word boundary or underscore and uppercasing it. It is loosely the inverse of `underscore`, though round-tripping is not guaranteed for strings like `"IOError"`.

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen/dash. It is a simple one-liner wrapper around `str.replace` with no other transformation, making it useful for converting snake_case identifiers into kebab-case (e.g. `"puni_puni"` → `"puni-puni"`).

## humanize

Converts an internal, machine-oriented string into a readable English phrase suitable for display. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix if present, replaces all remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases the interior, and then capitalizes the very first character (e.g. `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"`, `"author_id"` → `"Author"`).

## ordinal

Returns just the ordinal suffix string (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer, without attaching it to the number itself. It handles the special teen cases (11, 12, 13 always get `"th"`) and works correctly for negative numbers by taking the absolute value first (e.g. `ordinal(1)` → `"st"`, `ordinal(-11)` → `"th"`).

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string (e.g. `1` → `"1st"`, `1002` → `"1002nd"`). It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and then concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with the result.

## parameterize

Transforms an arbitrary string into a URL-safe "slug" suitable for use in a pretty URL. It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to their ASCII approximations, then replaces any remaining non-alphanumeric characters (other than hyphens and underscores) with the given separator (default `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and lowercases the result (e.g. `"Donald E. Knuth"` → `"donald-e-knuth"`).

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by applying the first matching rule from the module-level `PLURALS` list. Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (e.g. `"sheep"`, `"fish"`) are returned unchanged, as is the empty string. The rules handle common irregular forms, Latin-derived endings, and a catch-all append-`"s"` fallback (e.g. `"octopus"` → `"octopi"`, `"CamelOctopus"` → `"CamelOctopi"`).

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word by applying the first matching rule from the `SINGULARS` list, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. Words found in `UNCOUNTABLES` are returned as-is regardless of their form. The rules cover irregular plurals, Latin endings, and a generic strip-trailing-`"s"` fallback (e.g. `"octopi"` → `"octopus"`, `"posts"` → `"post"`).

## tableize

Derives a database table name from a class or model name using Rails conventions: it first converts the word to snake_case via `underscore`, then pluralizes the result via `pluralize` (e.g. `"RawScaledScorer"` → `"raw_scaled_scorers"`, `"fancyCategory"` → `"fancy_categories"`).

## titleize

Converts a string into title case suitable for display as a heading, capitalizing the first letter of every word. It normalizes the input by first running it through `underscore` and `humanize` (handling CamelCase, underscores, and `_id` suffixes), then applying Python's `str.title` followed by a regex pass that correctly capitalizes words following apostrophes (e.g. `"TheManWithoutAPast"` → `"The Man Without A Past"`, `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` → `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`).

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII Unicode characters with their closest ASCII equivalents by decomposing the string using Unicode NFKD normalization and then dropping any byte that cannot be encoded in ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently omitted rather than replaced with a placeholder (e.g. `"älämölö"` → `"alamolo"`, `"Ærøskøbing"` → `"rskbing"`).

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed-case string into a lowercase, underscore-separated (snake_case) form. It inserts underscores between runs of uppercase letters and at transitions from a lowercase letter or digit to an uppercase letter, then replaces any hyphens with underscores and lowercases the whole string (e.g. `"DeviceType"` → `"device_type"`). It is the approximate inverse of `camelize`.

# camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string to CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g. `"DeviceType"`). It works by replacing each character that follows an underscore (or the start of the string) with its uppercase equivalent, making it roughly the inverse of `underscore`, though the two are not perfect inverses for strings like `"IOError"`.

# dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen/dash and returns the result. It is a thin wrapper around `str.replace` with no other transformation applied, so `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`.

# humanize

Turns a raw identifier string into a readable, sentence-style label. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix if present, converts remaining underscores to spaces, lowercases all alphabetic characters, and then capitalises the very first character. The result is suitable for display in user interfaces where a single-sentence label is needed, for example `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"` or `"author_id"` → `"Author"`.

# ordinal

Returns the ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) appropriate for the given integer, handling the special cases for 11, 12, and 13 (which always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit). Negative numbers are treated by their absolute value, so `-1021` yields `"st"`. The function returns only the suffix, not the number itself; see `ordinalize` for the combined form.

# ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a string like `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"1003rd"`, or `"-11th"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and then concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with that suffix.

# parameterize

Transforms an arbitrary string into a URL-safe slug. It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to their closest ASCII equivalents, then replaces any character that is not alphanumeric, a hyphen, or an underscore with the separator (default `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and lowercases the whole result. For example, `"Donald E. Knuth"` becomes `"donald-e-knuth"`.

# pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by working through a prioritised list of regex substitution rules (`PLURALS`). Words in the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (such as `"sheep"` or `"fish"`) are returned unchanged. The rules handle common irregular patterns (octopus → octopi, quiz → quizzes, etc.) as well as the general English pluralisation rules, and case is preserved in the output.

# singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word is an uncountable noun and returns it unchanged if so, then applies the first matching rule from the `SINGULARS` list. Like `pluralize`, it preserves the case of the input word and handles common irregular forms such as `"octopi"` → `"octopus"`.

# tableize

Converts a class-style name into the lowercase, pluralised, underscore-separated table name that Rails would use for the corresponding database table. It does this by first applying `underscore` to convert CamelCase to snake_case and then `pluralize` to make the last word plural, so `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

# titleize

Produces a title-cased version of a string suitable for use as a heading. It first normalises the input through `underscore` and `humanize` (splitting camel-case words and replacing underscores with spaces), calls Python's built-in `.title()` for an initial pass, and then re-capitalises each word boundary match to ensure consistent results. For example, `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` becomes `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"` and `"TheManWithoutAPast"` becomes `"The Man Without A Past"`.

# transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII characters in a Unicode string with their closest ASCII approximations by first normalising the string to NFKD form (which decomposes accented characters into base letter plus combining mark) and then encoding to ASCII while dropping any character that has no ASCII equivalent. For example, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"`, and characters with no ASCII counterpart (like `Æ`) are silently removed.

# underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed-case string into a lowercase, underscore-separated (snake_case) form. It inserts an underscore between sequences of uppercase letters and a following capitalised word, between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter, and replaces hyphens with underscores, finishing with a full lowercase conversion. It is roughly the inverse of `camelize`, though the two are not perfectly invertible for strings like `"IOError"`.

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word (or its tail) matches any entry in `UNCOUNTABLES` and returns it unchanged if so, then works through the `SINGULARS` rule list and applies the first matching substitution. Words that are already singular and match no rule are returned as-is.

## tableize

Derives a database table name from a class or model name, following Ruby on Rails conventions. It pipes the input through `underscore` (converting CamelCase to snake_case) and then `pluralize`, so `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

## titleize

Produces a title-cased string suitable for display headings. It first normalizes the input through `underscore` and `humanize`, then capitalizes the first letter of every word (including those after punctuation such as colons). For example, `"x-men: the last stand"` becomes `"X Men: The Last Stand"` and `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` becomes `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`.

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII Unicode characters with their closest ASCII equivalents by decomposing the string using Unicode NFKD normalization and then dropping any byte that cannot be encoded as ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently removed. For example, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"`, while `"Ærøskøbing"` loses the characters that have no ASCII decomposition and becomes `"rskbing"`.

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed-case string to a lowercase, underscore-separated form. It inserts underscores between sequences of uppercase letters followed by a capitalized word, between a lowercase letter or digit and an uppercase letter, and replaces hyphens with underscores. For example, `"DeviceType"` becomes `"device_type"`. It is roughly the inverse of `camelize`, though the two are not perfect inverses for all inputs.

# inflection.py — Public Function Notes

## camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string to CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g. `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g. `"deviceType"`). It works by replacing each underscore-preceded character (and the first character when uppercasing) with its uppercase equivalent using a regex substitution.

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen-dash. It is a thin wrapper around `str.replace('_', '-')` with no other transformation, so case and all other characters are left unchanged (e.g. `"puni_puni"` → `"puni-puni"`).

## humanize

Turns a database-style identifier into a readable English phrase. It strips a trailing `_id` suffix, replaces remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases the entire string, then capitalizes only the first letter (e.g. `"employee_salary"` → `"Employee salary"`, `"author_id"` → `"Author"`). It is intended for generating friendly display labels rather than machine-readable strings.

## ordinal

Returns the correct English ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer, without attaching it to the number itself. It handles the special "teens" exception (11th, 12th, 13th) and works correctly for negative numbers by taking the absolute value before applying the rules.

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a complete ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, or `"1003rd"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal()` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with the result.

## parameterize

Transforms an arbitrary Unicode string into a URL-safe slug. It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to ASCII approximations via `transliterate()`, then replaces runs of any characters outside `[a-z0-9\-_]` with the separator (default `"-"`), collapses consecutive separators into one, strips leading and trailing separators, and lowercases the result (e.g. `"Donald E. Knuth"` → `"donald-e-knuth"`).

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word by testing the word against an ordered list of regex rules in `PLURALS`. It returns the word unchanged if it is empty or belongs to the `UNCOUNTABLES` set (words like "sheep" or "information" that have no distinct plural). The rules are applied in order and the first match wins, preserving the original capitalisation of the word.

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the inverse of `pluralize`. It first checks the word against `UNCOUNTABLES` using a whole-word, case-insensitive match and returns it unchanged if found; otherwise it walks the `SINGULARS` rule list and returns the result of the first matching substitution, again preserving original capitalisation.

## tableize

Derives a Rails-style database table name from a class or model name. It converts the input to snake_case via `underscore()` and then pluralizes the result via `pluralize()`, mirroring the convention Rails uses to map model class names to table names (e.g. `"RawScaledScorer"` → `"raw_scaled_scorers"`).

## titleize

Converts a string to title case suitable for display as a heading. It first normalizes the string through `underscore()` and `humanize()` to handle CamelCase and underscores, then capitalizes the first letter of every word. The result is intended for human-readable output, not for programmatic use (e.g. `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` → `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`).

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII Unicode characters with their closest ASCII equivalents by applying Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes accented characters into base letter + combining mark) and then stripping any byte that does not encode as ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently dropped (e.g. `"älämölö"` → `"alamolo"`).

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed string into a lowercase, underscore-separated form. It inserts underscores between sequences of uppercase letters and between an uppercase letter following a lowercase letter or digit, replaces hyphens with underscores, and lowercases the whole string (e.g. `"DeviceType"` → `"device_type"`). It is roughly the inverse of `camelize`, though the round-trip is not always perfect for strings like `"IOError"`.

artifact_text:
# inflection.py — Public Function Summaries

## camelize

Converts an underscored or dash-separated string into CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g., `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g., `"device_type"` → `"deviceType"`). It works by finding each character that follows an underscore (or the start of the string) and uppercasing it, then stripping the underscore. It is roughly the inverse of `underscore`, though round-tripping through both is not always lossless (e.g., `"IOError"` becomes `"IoError"` after `camelize(underscore(...))`).

## dasherize

Replaces every underscore in a string with a hyphen/dash. It is a thin wrapper around `str.replace('_', '-')` and performs no other transformation, so casing and all other characters are left untouched. For example, `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`.

## humanize

Converts a machine-style identifier into a readable, sentence-case label intended for display. It strips a trailing `"_id"` suffix if present, replaces all remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases the entire result, then capitalizes only the very first letter. For example, `"employee_salary"` becomes `"Employee salary"` and `"author_id"` becomes `"Author"`.

## ordinal

Returns the correct English ordinal suffix (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer without attaching it to the number itself. It handles the special cases for 11, 12, and 13 (which always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit), and operates on the absolute value of the input so negative numbers work correctly. For example, `ordinal(1)` → `"st"` and `ordinal(-11)` → `"th"`.

## ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"1003rd"`, or `"-11th"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number (preserving its sign) with the result. Unlike `ordinal`, the output includes the number itself.

## parameterize

Transforms a string into a URL-safe "slug" suitable for use in a pretty URL. It first transliterates non-ASCII characters to their closest ASCII equivalents via `transliterate`, then replaces any sequence of characters that are not alphanumeric, hyphens, or underscores with the separator (default `"-"`). Consecutive separators are collapsed to one, and any leading or trailing separators are stripped before the whole string is lowercased. For example, `"Donald E. Knuth"` becomes `"donald-e-knuth"`.

## pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word using a prioritized list of regex rules (`PLURALS`) and a set of known uncountable words (`UNCOUNTABLES`). If the word is empty or already in the uncountables set (e.g., `"sheep"`, `"information"`), it is returned unchanged. Otherwise, the rules are tried in order and the first matching rule's substitution is applied, handling irregular forms (such as `"octopus"` → `"octopi"`) and preserving the original casing of the word.

## singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word matches any entry in `UNCOUNTABLES` (using a word-boundary regex to avoid false matches inside longer words) and returns it unchanged if so. Otherwise it walks through the `SINGULARS` rule list in order, applies the first matching substitution, and returns the result. If no rule matches, the word is returned as-is.

## tableize

Converts a class-name-style string into a Rails-convention database table name: it first applies `underscore` to turn camel-case into snake_case, then applies `pluralize` to the result. For example, `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"` and `"fancyCategory"` becomes `"fancy_categories"`.

## titleize

Converts a string into a title-cased form suitable for human-readable headings. It first normalizes the input by running it through `underscore` (to handle camel-case) and then `humanize` (to replace underscores with spaces and strip `_id` suffixes), and finally capitalizes the first letter of every word. Non-alphanumeric word separators such as hyphens are replaced by spaces in the process; for example, `"x-men: the last stand"` becomes `"X Men: The Last Stand"`.

## transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII characters in a Unicode string with their closest ASCII approximations by applying Unicode NFKD normalization (which decomposes characters into base letters plus combining marks) and then stripping any bytes that cannot be encoded as ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently dropped rather than substituted. For example, `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"` and `"Ærøskøbing"` becomes `"rskbing"`.

## underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed identifier string into a lowercase, underscore-separated (snake_case) form. It inserts underscores between consecutive uppercase letters and the following capitalized word (e.g., `"XMLParser"` → `"xml_parser"`), between a lowercase letter or digit and a following uppercase letter, and converts any existing hyphens to underscores before lowercasing the whole string. It is roughly the inverse of `camelize`, though round-tripping is not always lossless.

# camelize

Converts an underscore-separated string to CamelCase. By default it produces UpperCamelCase (e.g., `"device_type"` → `"DeviceType"`), but passing `uppercase_first_letter=False` yields lowerCamelCase instead (e.g., `"deviceType"`). It works by uppercasing the first letter after each underscore (and the first letter of the string when using the default mode), making it the rough inverse of `underscore`.

# dasherize

Replaces every underscore character in a string with a hyphen-dash. It is a thin wrapper around `str.replace` with no other transformation, so `"puni_puni"` becomes `"puni-puni"`.

# humanize

Turns a snake_case identifier into a readable English phrase suitable for display. It strips a trailing `_id` suffix, replaces remaining underscores with spaces, lowercases everything, and then capitalizes the very first character — so `"employee_salary"` becomes `"Employee salary"` and `"author_id"` becomes `"Author"`.

# ordinal

Returns the ordinal suffix string (`"st"`, `"nd"`, `"rd"`, or `"th"`) for a given integer, handling the special teen cases (11, 12, 13) that always take `"th"` regardless of their last digit. It does not include the number itself; use `ordinalize` for that.

# ordinalize

Combines a number with its ordinal suffix to produce a full ordinal string such as `"1st"`, `"2nd"`, `"1003rd"`, or `"-11th"`. It delegates suffix selection to `ordinal` and simply concatenates the original number with the result.

# parameterize

Converts a string into a URL-friendly "slug" by first transliterating non-ASCII characters to ASCII approximations, then replacing any remaining non-alphanumeric characters with a separator (default `"-"`), collapsing runs of the separator, and stripping it from both ends before lowercasing the result. For example, `"Donald E. Knuth"` becomes `"donald-e-knuth"`.

# pluralize

Returns the plural form of an English word using a prioritized list of regex substitution rules stored in `PLURALS`, while leaving uncountable words (like `"sheep"` or `"rice"`) unchanged. It applies the first matching rule and preserves the original case pattern of the word.

# singularize

Returns the singular form of an English word, acting as the reverse of `pluralize`. It first checks whether the word is uncountable and returns it as-is if so, then walks through the `SINGULARS` regex rule list and applies the first match, again preserving case.

# tableize

Produces a Rails-style database table name from a class or model name by first converting the string to snake_case with `underscore` and then pluralizing the result with `pluralize`. For example, `"RawScaledScorer"` becomes `"raw_scaled_scorers"`.

# titleize

Converts a string into a title-cased human-readable heading by first underscoring it, then humanizing it, then capitalizing the first letter of every word. It handles CamelCase identifiers, underscored names, and hyphenated strings alike — for instance, `"raiders_of_the_lost_ark"` becomes `"Raiders Of The Lost Ark"`.

# transliterate

Replaces non-ASCII characters in a Unicode string with their closest ASCII equivalents by decomposing the string using Unicode NFKD normalization and then dropping any byte that cannot be encoded as ASCII. Characters with no ASCII approximation are silently removed, so `"älämölö"` becomes `"alamolo"` and `"Ærøskøbing"` becomes `"rskbing"`.

# underscore

Converts a CamelCase or mixed identifier string into a lowercase snake_case string. It inserts underscores between sequences of uppercase letters and between a lowercase letter followed by an uppercase letter, replaces hyphens with underscores, and lowercases the entire result — making it the rough inverse of `camelize`.
